content.text: Embedding provider APIs accept text and return dense floating-point vectors that encode semantic meaning. The three major cloud providers — OpenAI, Cohere, and Voyage AI — each expose slightly different models, dimension options, batching limits, and per-token pricing. Choosing the right one depends on your language requirements, storage budget, retrieval accuracy targets, and cost constraints.

attrs.alt: Embedding provider APIs comparison: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large/small, Cohere embed-v4/english-v3, Voyage voyage-3-large/lite — covering dimensions, batch limits, input types, and cost per million tokens. Bottom shows batching code pattern and model selection guide.

content.text: You send an HTTP POST with a list of strings; the provider encodes each string through a transformer model and returns a list of float arrays in the same order. The key contract is that identical strings always produce identical vectors for a given model and dimension setting — this matters for caching and incremental indexing.

# pip install openai cohere voyageai
from openai import OpenAI
import cohere
import voyageai

# --- OpenAI ---
oai = OpenAI()  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from env

def embed_openai(texts: list[str], model="text-embedding-3-small") -> list[list[float]]:
    response = oai.embeddings.create(model=model, input=texts)
    return [item.embedding for item in response.data]  # order preserved


# --- Cohere ---
co = cohere.Client()  # reads COHERE_API_KEY

def embed_cohere_documents(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
    response = co.embed(
        texts=texts,
        model="embed-v4.0",
        input_type="search_document",  # required for Cohere
        embedding_types=["float"],
    )
    return response.embeddings.float

def embed_cohere_query(query: str) -> list[float]:
    response = co.embed(
        texts=[query],
        model="embed-v4.0",
        input_type="search_query",
        embedding_types=["float"],
    )
    return response.embeddings.float[0]


# --- Voyage AI ---
vo = voyageai.Client()  # reads VOYAGE_API_KEY

def embed_voyage_documents(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
    result = vo.embed(texts, model="voyage-3-large", input_type="document")
    return result.embeddings

def embed_voyage_query(query: str) -> list[float]:
    result = vo.embed([query], model="voyage-3-large", input_type="query")
    return result.embeddings[0]

Cohere and Voyage require you to specify whether each text is a query or a document. This is not cosmetic — the model is asymmetrically trained so that query embeddings and document embeddings are comparable in the same vector space even though they are produced by different forward passes. OpenAI's models do not expose this distinction; they treat all inputs identically, which is simpler but gives up some retrieval accuracy for asymmetric tasks.
used when embedding text that goes into your index. Call this at index-build time.
used when embedding the user's question at retrieval time. Must use the same model as the documents.
Cohere supports additional types that tune the embedding for downstream classification rather than retrieval.

Embedding one text at a time is 50–100x slower than batching. Each API call has a fixed per-call overhead; batching amortizes it across many texts. Always batch when indexing more than a handful of documents.

import time
from openai import OpenAI, RateLimitError

client = OpenAI()

def embed_batch(
    texts: list[str],
    model: str = "text-embedding-3-small",
    batch_size: int = 512,
    max_retries: int = 5,
) -> list[list[float]]:
    """Embed texts in batches with exponential backoff on rate limits."""
    vectors: list[list[float]] = []

    for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size):
        chunk = texts[i : i + batch_size]
        delay = 1.0
        for attempt in range(max_retries):
            try:
                resp = client.embeddings.create(model=model, input=chunk)
                vectors.extend(item.embedding for item in resp.data)
                break
            except RateLimitError:
                if attempt == max_retries - 1:
                    raise
                time.sleep(delay)
                delay *= 2

    return vectors  # same order as input


# Embed 10,000 docs: ~20 API calls instead of 10,000
docs = [f"Document {i} content here." for i in range(10_000)]
vectors = embed_batch(docs, model="text-embedding-3-small", batch_size=512)
print(f"{len(vectors)} vectors, dim={len(vectors[0])}")

OpenAI's text-embedding-3 models support Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL): you can truncate the full 3072-dimensional vector to any smaller number of dimensions while preserving most of the semantic information. This is not the same as PCA — the model is explicitly trained so that the first
dimensions of the full vector are already a good representation at dimension

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

# Full 3072-dim vector
full = client.embeddings.create(
    model="text-embedding-3-large",
    input=["What is machine learning?"],
)
print(len(full.data[0].embedding))  # 3072

# Reduced to 256 dimensions via API parameter (no post-processing needed)
reduced = client.embeddings.create(
    model="text-embedding-3-large",
    input=["What is machine learning?"],
    dimensions=256,
)
print(len(reduced.data[0].embedding))  # 256

# Storage impact:
# 3072 dims x 4 bytes (float32) = 12,288 bytes per vector
# 256 dims  x 4 bytes           = 1,024  bytes per vector  (12x smaller)
# For 1M documents: 12 GB → 1 GB  (significant for RAM or vector DB cost)
#
# Accuracy at 256-dim is roughly equal to text-embedding-ada-002 at full 1536-dim.
# Benchmark on your dataset before committing to reduced dimensions.

Most providers return L2-normalized vectors (unit vectors). When vectors are normalized, cosine similarity and dot product give the same ranking, so you can use whichever your vector database supports more efficiently. If you use Voyage or a self-hosted model that does not normalize, normalize explicitly before indexing.

import numpy as np


def normalize(vectors: list[list[float]]) -> list[list[float]]:
    arr = np.array(vectors, dtype=np.float32)
    norms = np.linalg.norm(arr, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    norms = np.maximum(norms, 1e-12)  # avoid division by zero
    return (arr / norms).tolist()


def cosine_similarity(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
    va = np.array(a)
    vb = np.array(b)
    return float(np.dot(va, vb) / (np.linalg.norm(va) * np.linalg.norm(vb)))


# OpenAI returns normalized vectors — no extra step needed:
vecs = embed_openai(["hello world"])
norm = np.linalg.norm(vecs[0])
print(f"OpenAI vector norm: {norm:.4f}")  # 1.0000

# If mixing providers: normalize all vectors before indexing
# to ensure cosine distances are comparable across batches.
When indexing millions of documents, the sequential batch loop becomes the bottleneck. Use asyncio to issue multiple batches concurrently, bounded by the provider's rate limit.

import asyncio
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

async_client = AsyncOpenAI()

SEMAPHORE = asyncio.Semaphore(10)  # 10 concurrent requests


async def embed_chunk_async(
    chunk: list[str], model: str
) -> list[list[float]]:
    async with SEMAPHORE:
        resp = await async_client.embeddings.create(model=model, input=chunk)
        return [item.embedding for item in resp.data]


async def embed_all_async(
    texts: list[str],
    model: str = "text-embedding-3-small",
    batch_size: int = 512,
) -> list[list[float]]:
    chunks = [
        texts[i : i + batch_size] for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size)
    ]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[embed_chunk_async(c, model) for c in chunks])
    # Flatten while preserving order
    return [vec for batch in results for vec in batch]


# Usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    docs = [f"Document content {i}" for i in range(50_000)]
    vectors = asyncio.run(embed_all_async(docs))
    print(f"{len(vectors)} vectors embedded")
    # ~10x faster than sequential for 50k docs

Embedding the same text twice costs twice as much. For content that changes rarely — product descriptions, knowledge base articles, documentation — cache the vectors by a hash of the source text. The cache key is the SHA-256 of the exact string; if the text changes, the hash changes and you re-embed.
good default. Strong quality, cheap, supports dimension reduction, simple API with no input-type distinction. Use for English-heavy retrieval.
best choice when you need multimodal embeddings (text + images in the same space) or strong multilingual support across 100 languages.
highest retrieval accuracy for English RAG based on MTEB benchmarks. Worth the higher cost when retrieval precision is the primary metric.
Always benchmark on your own data:
MTEB leaderboard rankings are averages across many datasets. For your specific domain and query patterns, the ordering may differ by 5–15 points.
